---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
incremental: false
header-includes: |
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, backgrounds, calc}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
  \usepackage{xcolor}
  \definecolor{mblue}{RGB}{35,55,100}
---

```{=latex}
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\
\vspace{0.5em}
{\normalsize 12. Correlational studies and causal inference}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}
\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\hfill \includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
}
\maketitle
```


# 3.1 Correlation vs Causality

## The Premise

Fundamentally, in science we are always interested in understanding causal mechanisms.

This allows us to understand how to **intervene** and produce **changes**.


## The Fundamental Problem

\small

Observational clinical research produces statistical associations.

But is an association sufficient to justify a therapeutic intervention?

Is an association sufficient to establish a cause-effect relationship?

## Correlation is not causation

![](../Figures/corrCause1.png){width="80%" fig-align="center"}

## The Problem

Often the problem of possible confusion between correlation and cause is trivialized because the examples used to explain it are extreme (it is easy to identify the fallacy)

Many times the fallacy is nonetheless committed in the interpretation of results, _especially if the causal relationship is plausible or related to other evidence_.


## Important Examples

**Neuropsychological example**: In many cases the correlation between education and different trajectories of cognitive decline has been interpreted with a "protective" role

![](../Figures/Fjell_2025.png){width="70%" fig-align="center"}

## A contradictory result

![](../Figures/Fjell_results.png){width="80%" fig-align="center"}

## Different relationships, same observed effect
If we were to analyze the relationship between X and Y, we would find in these three cases a strong correlation (or "prediction" via regression) between X and Y.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!80,font=\scriptsize,
               minimum size=0.55cm,inner sep=1pt, text=white},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]

% ── Case 1 ──────────────────────────────────────────────
\node[font=\tiny\bfseries] at (-5,3.2) {Case 1 - Direct cause};
\node[node] (X)  at (-5.7,2.3) {X};
\node[node] (Y)  at (-4.3,2.3) {Y};
\draw[arr] (X)--(Y);

% ── Case 2 ──────────────────────────────────────────────
\node[font=\tiny\bfseries] at (-2,3.2) {Case 2 - Confounder};
\node[node] (Z)  at (-2,2.6)  {Z};
\node[node] (X2) at (-2.8,1.9) {X};
\node[node] (Y2) at (-1.2,1.9) {Y};
\draw[arr] (Z)--(X2); \draw[arr] (Z)--(Y2);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (-2,0.9) {Z causes both X and Y\\(spurious assoc.\ X--Y)};

% ── Case 3 ──────────────────────────────────────────────
\node[font=\tiny\bfseries] at (0.8,3.2) {Case 3 - Mediator};
\node[node] (Z2) at (0.8,2.6)  {Z};
\node[node] (X3) at (0.0,1.9)  {X};
\node[node] (Y3) at (1.6,1.9)  {Y};
\draw[arr] (X3)--(Z2); \draw[arr] (Z2)--(Y3);
\draw[arr] (X3)--(Y3);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (0.8,0.9) {X influences Z and Y.\\Z mediates X on Y.};

% ── Case 4 ──────────────────────────────────────────────
\node[font=\tiny\bfseries] at (-5,0.2) {Case 4 - Reverse cause};
\node[node] (X4) at (-5.7,-0.6) {X};
\node[node] (Y4) at (-4.3,-0.6) {Y};
\draw[arr] (Y4)--(X4);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (-5,-1.4) {Y causes X, not vice versa)};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Statistical problems in studying associations between variables.

- **Collinearity**: if I have two variables that are highly correlated, I might not be able to distinguish their effects and including both in a model can create distortions.

- **Selection bias**: who enters my sample might not be representative of the population (e.g. _self-selection_), dropout might be related to a relevant variable, collider bias (see below).

- **Direction of the relationship**: it is rarely easy to determine the direction of the relationship (e.g., do I have high cognitive scores because I studied a lot or did I study a lot because I have high cognitive scores?)

- **Measurement error**: let us always remember that there is measurement error that could also be different among the tests used.




## Toward causality: The Bradford Hill Criteria

**Bradford Hill Criteria** (1965): 9 criteria for assessing evidence of causality in epidemiology (strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, dose-response, biological plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy).

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Bradford Hill (1965). The environment and disease: association or causation? \emph{Proc R Soc Med} 58:295--300. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576505800503}

## The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference

\small

The **Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference** (Holland, 1986): we can never observe both **counterfactuals** for the same individual at the same time.

\vspace{0.4em}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!20,text width=3.8cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=1cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (paz) at (0,2.5) {Patient with MCI};
\node[box] (t1)  at (-2.8,0.8) {Receives cognitive\\training};
\node[box] (t0)  at ( 2.8,0.8) {Does not receive\\cognitive training};
\node[box] (y1)  at (-2.8,-0.6) {Outcome $Y^{(1)}$\\(observed)};
\node[box] (y0)  at ( 2.8,-0.6) {Outcome $Y^{(0)}$\\(\textbf{counterfactual})};
\draw[arr] (paz)--(t1); \draw[arr] (paz)--(t0);
\draw[arr] (t1)--(y1);  \draw[arr] (t0)--(y0);
\node[font=\tiny,red!70] at (2.8,-1.4) {Not observable!};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

\small The individual causal effect = $Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)}$. But we observe only one of the two. Randomization solves the problem \emph{on average} between groups.

# 3.2 DAG: Directed Acyclic Graphs

## What is a DAG?

\small

A **DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a tool for explicitly representing **causal assumptions** about a system. Each arrow represents a hypothesis of direct causal relationship.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Components**:
\footnotesize
- **Nodes**: variables (observed or latent)
- **Arrows**: direct causal relationships
- **Acyclic**: no cycles (A $\to$ B $\to$ A)

\vspace{0.3em}
**What it is for**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Identify which variables to control for (and which \emph{not} to control for)
\item Identify confounders, mediators, colliders
\item Assess whether a causal effect is identifiable from available data
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Textor et al.\ (2016). Robust causal reasoning using DAGs. \emph{Int J Epidemiol}.\\
Online tool: DAGitty --- Open Access: \url{https://www.dagitty.net}


## Fundamental Structures in DAGs

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=0.65cm,inner sep=1pt},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
% Chain (mediator)
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (-4,3.5) {Chain (Mediator)};
\node[node] (A1) at (-5,2.5) {A};
\node[node] (M)  at (-4,2.5) {M};
\node[node] (B1) at (-3,2.5) {B};
\draw[arr] (A1)--(M); \draw[arr] (M)--(B1);
\node[font=\tiny] at (-4,1.9) {A $\to$ M $\to$ B};

% Fork (confounder)
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (0,3.5) {Fork (Confounder)};
\node[node] (C)  at (0,2.8) {C};
\node[node] (A2) at (-1,2.0) {A};
\node[node] (B2) at (1,2.0)  {B};
\draw[arr] (C)--(A2); \draw[arr] (C)--(B2);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (0,1) {C causes both A and B\\(spurious association A--B)};

% Collider
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (4,3.5) {Collider};
\node[node] (A3) at (3,2.8) {A};
\node[node] (B3) at (5,2.8) {B};
\node[node] (K)  at (4,2.0) {K};
\draw[arr] (A3)--(K); \draw[arr] (B3)--(K);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (4,1) {Both A and B cause K\\(independent, but conditioned on K:\\spurious association!)};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Confounder: Neuropsychological Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  obs/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!50,text=white,font=\footnotesize,
              minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},red!60,dashed,thick}
]
\node[obs] (eta)  at (0,3)   {Age};
\node[obs] (dep)  at (-2.5,1) {Worse Memory};
\node[obs] (cog)  at ( 2.5,1) {AD Risk};
\draw[arr]     (eta)--(dep);
\draw[arr]     (eta)--(cog);
\draw[spurious](dep)--(cog)
  node[midway,below,font=\tiny,red!70]{Spurious association?};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small Age causes both depression and cognitive decline. If we do not control for age, we find a depression--cognition association that could be \textbf{entirely} or \textbf{partially} spurious.

\vspace{0.3em}
\textbf{Solution}: add age as a covariate in regression --- or better, build the DAG \emph{before} analyzing the data.

## Collider: A Subtle Error

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},red!60,dashed,very thick}
]
\node[node] (gen) at (-2.5,2) {Genetic\\factors\\(APOE)};
\node[node] (tbi) at ( 2.5,2) {Age};
\node[node] (ad)  at (0,0.5)  {AD\\diagnosis};
\draw[arr] (gen)--(ad);
\draw[arr] (tbi)--(ad);
\draw[spurious] (gen) to[bend left=25] node[above,font=\tiny,red!70, align=center]{Negative association\\(collider bias!)} (tbi);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small In a clinical sample (selected for having AD), APOE and Age become **negatively correlated** even though in the general population they are independent. \textbf{Conditioning on a collider opens a spurious path}.

\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Practical implication}: studies conducted only on hospitalized patients or with a specific diagnosis can suffer from collider bias (also known as \emph{Berkson's bias}).


## Collider (covariate to not include)

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},red!60,dashed,very thick}
]
\node[node, align=center] (gen) at (-2.5,2) {Language\\Abilities};
\node[node] (tbi) at ( 2.5,2) {Age};
\node[node] (ad)  at (0,0.5)  {Global Cognitive\\Functioning};
\draw[arr] (gen)--(ad);
\draw[arr] (tbi)--(ad);
\draw[spurious] (gen) to[bend left=25] node[above,font=\tiny,red!70, align=center]{Negative association\\(collider bias!)} (tbi);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small Controlling for a **collider** risks having biased estimates (e.g., including a covariate in a regression model)


## Mediator: Not Always to Be Controlled

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[node] (dep)  at (-2.5,1.5) {Depression};
\node[node] (som)  at (0,1.5)    {Insomnia};
\node[node] (att)  at (2.5,1.5)  {Attention\\(worsened)};
\draw[arr] (dep)--(som);
\draw[arr] (som)--(att);
\draw[arr] (dep) to[bend right=30] (att);
\node[font=\tiny,mblue] at (0,0.3) {Direct + indirect (mediated) path};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small If we want to estimate the **total** effect of depression on attention, \textbf{we must not} control for insomnia (which is a mediator). Doing so would block part of the causal effect.

\vspace{0.3em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Direct vs indirect effect}: mediation analysis (e.g., via SEM or packages such as \texttt{mediation} in R) allows us to decompose the total effect.



# 3.3 Methods for Causal Inference from Observational Data

## Propensity Score

\small

The **propensity score** (Rosenbaum \& Rubin, 1983) is the probability that a subject receives treatment, conditional on their observed characteristics.

$$PS_i = P(\text{Treatment}_i = 1 \mid X_i)$$

where $X_i$ is the vector of observed covariates.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Intuition**: subjects with the same propensity score are "quasi-randomized" with respect to observed covariates.

\vspace{0.4em}

## How are Propensity Scores Used?

**Methods of use**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{Matching}: match treated and control subjects with similar PS
\item \textbf{Stratification}: divide into propensity score quintiles
\item \textbf{Weighting} (IPW): weights inversely proportional to PS
\item \textbf{Covariate}: include PS as a covariate in regression
\end{itemize}



## Propensity Score: Neuropsychological Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!15,text width=3.5cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.9cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (cov) at (0,4)  {Covariates:\\age, education,\\comorbidities, MMSE};
\node[box] (ps)  at (0,2.5)  {Estimate PS:\\logistic / random forest};
\node[box] (mat) at (0,1)  {Matching or\\IPW weighting};
\node[box] (out) at (0,-0.5) {Estimate causal\\effect of treatment};
\draw[arr] (cov)--(ps); \draw[arr] (ps)--(mat); \draw[arr] (mat)--(out);
\node[right,font=\tiny,text width=3.5cm, align=center] at (2.0,3.5) {E.g.\ patients who\\chose training\\vs no treatment};
\node[right,font=\tiny,text width=3.5cm, align=center] at (2.0,1) {Balances\\pre-treatment\\characteristics};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

\small \textbf{Key assumption (not verifiable!)}: \emph{no unmeasured confounders}: all variables that influence both treatment selection and outcome have been measured and included in the PS.

## Limitations of Propensity Score

\small

**Assumption of positivity**: every subject must have probability $> 0$ of receiving each treatment. If some subjects have PS $= 0$ or $= 1$, comparison is not possible.

\vspace{0.3em}

**No unmeasured confounders**: the PS balances observed covariates, but not latent or unmeasured ones (e.g., patient motivation, undetected family support).

\vspace{0.3em}

**Insufficient overlap**: if treated and control subjects are very different, matching does not find adequate pairs and estimation is unreliable.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Not an RCT**: the PS reduces bias from observed confounding, but does not eliminate it completely. It should always be accompanied by a sensitivity analysis.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Austin (2011). An introduction to propensity score methods. \emph{Multivariate Behav Res} 46(3). Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1080/00273171.2011.568786}

<!--
## Other Approaches to Causal Inference

\small

**Instrumental Variables (IV)**

An instrumental variable $Z$ influences treatment but not outcome except through treatment. It allows estimating causal effects even in the presence of unmeasured confounders.

\emph{Example}: geographic variations in access to neurorehabilitation services as an instrument.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Difference-in-Differences (DiD)**

Compares the change over time between an exposed group and a non-exposed group, controlling for pre-existing trends.

\vspace{0.3em}

-->

## Propensity scores and Sensitivity Analysis

To probe the limitations of PS, several additional robustness analyses are often used.
One is the **Sensitivity Analysis**.

E-value analysis (VanderWeele \& Ding, 2017): quantifies how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain the observed association.

The higher the E-value, the more robust the propensity score analysis is

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny VanderWeele \& Ding (2017). Sensitivity analysis in observational research: introducing the E-value. \emph{Ann Intern Med}. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2607}

<!--
# 3.4 Theoretical Frameworks

## Potential Outcomes Framework (Rubin)

\small

The **potential outcomes** framework (Rubin, 1974; Holland, 1986) formalizes causality through counterfactual variables.

\vspace{0.3em}

For each subject $i$ and treatment $t \in \{0,1\}$:
$$Y_i^{(t)} = \text{outcome that would be observed if the subject received treatment } t$$

**Individual causal effect**: $\tau_i = Y_i^{(1)} - Y_i^{(0)}$

**Average Treatment Effect (ATE)**: $\tau = E[Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)}]$

**ATE in the treated (ATT)**: $\tau_{ATT} = E[Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)} \mid T=1]$

\vspace{0.3em}
\small Randomization ensures that $E[Y^{(0)} \mid T=1] = E[Y^{(0)} \mid T=0]$ (exchangeability), making the counterfactual identifiable.

-->
## Do-Calculus and Pearl's Framework

\small

Judea Pearl developed a framework based on DAGs and **do-calculus** to answer causal questions.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Fundamental difference**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item $P(Y \mid X=x)$: observed probability of $Y$ given that we observe $X=x$ (statistical)
\item $P(Y \mid do(X=x))$: probability of $Y$ if we \emph{intervene} by setting $X=x$ (causal)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Backdoor criterion**: a set of variables $Z$ blocks all "backdoor" paths between treatment and outcome $\Rightarrow$ controlling for $Z$ yields a causal estimate.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Practical tool**: **DAGitty** (\url{www.dagitty.net}) allows drawing DAGs online and automatically identifies the minimal adjustment set.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Pearl (2009). \emph{Causality}. Cambridge University Press.\\
Free: Pearl et al.\ (2016). \emph{Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer}. Wiley. Chapters available Open Access: \url{http://bayes.cs.ucla.edu/PRIMER/}


## Backdoor Criterion: Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  blocked/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!50,text=white,font=\footnotesize,
                  minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  back/.style={-{Stealth},red!50,dashed,thick}
]
\node[node]    (ansia) at (0,3.5)   {Anxiety};
\node[node]    (cann) at (-2.5,2)  {Cannabis Use};
\node[blocked] (cog) at (2.5,2)   {Cognition\\(Outcome)};

\draw[arr] (cann)--(cog) node[midway,above,font=\small]{?};
\draw[back] (ansia)--(cog);
\draw[back] (ansia)--(cann);
\draw[back] (cann) to[bend left=65] node[above,font=\tiny,red!60]{Backdoor path via anxiety} (ansia);

\node[font=\scriptsize,red!70,text width=5cm] at (0,-1.5)
  {Blocking age closes the backdoor path and allows causal estimation\\ depression $\to$ cognition};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Confusion Index: Summary of Structures

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{>{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Structure} & \textbf{Control?} & \textbf{Effect if controlled} \\
\midrule
Confounder (fork)  & \textbf{Yes}  & Removes spurious association \\
Mediator (chain)  & \textbf{No}  & Blocks part of the causal effect \\
Collider           & \textbf{No}  & Opens a spurious path \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\footnotesize \emph{NB: the choice depends on the causal question, not on statistical significance}} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}
```
\small
![](../Figures/triangle.png){width="10%"} In psychology and neuropsychology, inserting control variables "because they are correlated" without a DAG can produce worse estimates than no control at all.


## Summary: From Correlation to Causality

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!20,text width=3.4cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.85cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (q)  at (0,4) {Causal question\\(not just associative)};
\node[box] (dag) at (0,2.5) {DAG: represent\\causal assumptions};
\node[box] (id) at (0,1)  {Identification:\\backdoor criterion\\adjustment set};
\node[box] (est) at (0,-0.5) {Estimation:\\PS matching, IPW,\\IV, DiD, RD};
\node[box] (sen) at (0,-2){Sensitivity analysis:\\E-value, RD bounds};
\draw[arr] (q)--(dag); \draw[arr] (dag)--(id);
\draw[arr] (id)--(est); \draw[arr] (est)--(sen);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## How to solve? With appropriate models.

Among appropriate models, some are **Structural Equation Models** (SEM) which:

- explicitly model causal directions.
- account for measurement error and can model latent variables (e.g., Constructs) with multiple indicators (e.g., tests)

NOTE: Every SEM is implicitly a DAG

## What SEM Cannot Do

**SEM cannot**:

- **tell us the direction of causality** (we must hypothesize it ourselves; an SEM model with completely wrong direction can yield very strong results)

- **solve sampling problems** they will always influence the results.

-**give generalizable estimates if the sample is small** SEMs risk overfitting if the sample is not of adequate size.

## So then?

Can I conduct a study with simple correlations or multiple regression and at least make some hypotheses?

Yes, it can be useful but...
\pause

![](../Figures/Lewer_et_al_2025.png){width="80%" fig-align="center"}
\centering
\href{https://bmjmedicine.bmj.com/content/4/1/e001375}{\underline{\textbf{Link to OA article}}}


## Take-home message

Correlational studies can seem simple, but sometimes lead to misleading conclusions.

Again, remember to interpret results with judgment and be aware of the pitfalls of interpreting cause-effect relationships where they may not exist.


<!--
## Practical Open Access Tools — Section 3

\small

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize

\item \textbf{DAGitty} (online DAG, free):\\
  \url{https://www.dagitty.net}

\item \textbf{Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer} (Pearl et al.):
  Selected chapters Open Access:\\
  \url{http://bayes.cs.ucla.edu/PRIMER/}

\item \textbf{The Effect: An Introduction to Research Design and Causality}\\
  (Huntington-Klein, 2022). Entire book Open Access:\\
  \url{https://theeffectbook.net}

\item \textbf{Causal Inference: The Mixtape} (Cunningham, 2021).\\
  Open Access: \url{https://mixtape.scunning.com}

\item \textbf{Propensity Score}: Austin (2011). \emph{Multivariate Behav Res}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1080/00273171.2011.568786}

\item \textbf{E-value}: VanderWeele \& Ding (2017). \emph{Ann Intern Med}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2607}

\item \textbf{Bradford Hill criteria}: Hill (1965). \emph{Proc R Soc Med}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576505800503}

\item \textbf{R package \texttt{MatchIt}} (PS matching):
  Ho et al., Open Access:\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v042.i08}

\end{itemize}

-->
